title: AI Agents & Automation
subtitle: From simple tool use to multi-agent systems that reason, plan, and automate real business workflows
heroTitle: AI Agents & Automation

objectives.title: Level Up the Agent Equation
objectives.description: Go beyond Session 4's intro. Master advanced patterns: ReAct (Reason + Act), chain-of-thought planning, and multi-step orchestration.

objectives.title: Understand Multi-Agent Systems
objectives.description: See how specialized agents collaborate: one researches, one writes, one reviews. Learn when single-agent vs. multi-agent is the right call.

objectives.title: Build a Research Agent
objectives.description: Hands-on: build an agent that searches, evaluates sources, synthesizes findings, and produces a structured report.

objectives.title: Evaluate Agent ROI for Business
objectives.description: Apply a framework for deciding where AI agents create real value: which processes to automate, what stays human, and how to measure success.

agenda.title: Part 1: From Products to Agents
agenda.description: THE KEY INSIGHT: A chatbot answers questions. An agent DOES things. Let's meet the agent equation and the 500-line app that beat a 400,000-line codebase.
bulletPoints: Chatbot vs. Agent: One answers, the other acts. That distinction changes everything.
The Agent Equation: LLM + Tools + Reasoning Loop + Memory = Agent
NanoClaw vs OpenClaw: 500 lines of agent code beat 400,000 lines of traditional code. How?
Visual: nanoclaw-beginners-guide.html on projector — see the architecture at a glance
ACTION: Open the Session 4 Colab notebook, enter your API key. We're building an agent in 30 minutes.

agenda.title: Part 2: Build Your First Agent
agenda.description: Hands-on in Google Colab. You'll go from zero to a working multi-tool agent that decides for itself when and how to use tools.
bulletPoints: Open Session 4 Colab notebook. API key from Session 3 still works.
Single Tool (8 min): Give Claude a calculator tool — watch it decide when to use it and when to answer directly
Multi-Tool (10 min): Add stock price + company info tools. Watch Claude chain tools together to answer complex questions.
Experiment (12 min): Try your own questions. Ask 'What is the capital of France?' — Claude skips the tools entirely. It reasons about what it needs.
Key moment: You didn't program any logic. The LLM decided. That's the agent equation in action.

agenda.title: Part 3: What Makes Agents Powerful
agenda.description: You just built an agent. But it's naive — it uses tools once and stops. Here's what production agents do differently.
bulletPoints: ReAct (Reason + Act): Think → Act → Observe → Repeat. The pattern behind every serious agent.
LIVE: react-agent-example.html — Play the trace. Watch an agent reason through a research task step by step.
Multi-Agent architectures: Pipeline (sequential), Debate (adversarial), Orchestrator (manager + workers)
MCP (Model Context Protocol): The USB-C of AI — one standard for connecting agents to any tool, any data source
Visual: advanced-agent-patterns.html — ReAct, multi-agent, and MCP in one interactive diagram

agenda.title: Break
agenda.description: Stretch, grab coffee. You've gone from never building an agent to understanding production patterns. Next: you build a real one.

agenda.title: Part 4: Build a Research Agent
agenda.description: Hands-on in Google Colab. Build an agent that researches a topic, evaluates sources, and writes a structured report.
bulletPoints: Open Session 8 Colab notebook. Same API key, new notebook.
Phase 1 (8 min): Build 3 research tools — web_search, evaluate_source, extract_key_facts
Phase 2 (10 min): Wire the ReAct loop — agent decides what to search, evaluates results, searches again if needed
Phase 3 (12 min): Add report generation — agent synthesizes findings into a structured briefing
Challenge: Ask your agent to research a real business question. Does it find good sources? Does the report make sense?

agenda.title: Part 5: Business Automation & Agent ROI
agenda.description: Agents are impressive in demos. But where do they create real business value? MCP, automation strategy, and ROI.
bulletPoints: MCP ecosystem: 1000+ servers already — Slack, GitHub, Google Drive, Salesforce, databases
NanoClaw + MCP demo: See how a real agent connects to tools via the universal standard
The Automation Matrix: High volume + clear rules = automate. Low volume + judgment = augment.
Case studies: Invoice processing (full automation), sales outreach (agent + human), strategy (human + AI assist)
ROI framework: Time saved × hourly cost × error reduction − agent cost = monthly value
New roles emerging: Agent Architect, Prompt Operations Manager, AI Process Designer

agenda.title: Part 6: Wrap-Up + Homework
agenda.description: At 5:00 you'd never built an agent. At 6:50 you had a ReAct research agent that reasons, evaluates, and reports.
bulletPoints: The journey: Tool Use → Single Agent → ReAct Agent → Multi-Agent → Business Automation
Homework: Extend your research agent, design a multi-agent system, explore MCP servers, read Anthropic's agent guide
NanoClaw code walkthrough available as take-home reading — see how 500 lines of agent code actually work
Key question to carry forward: What should be autonomous, what needs a human, and who decides?
Next week: Ethics & Responsible AI — the guardrails that make all of this safe and trustworthy

resources.title: Anthropic Agent Documentation
resources.description: Official guide to building agentic systems with Claude. Covers tool use, multi-step reasoning, and best practices for production agents.

resources.title: Model Context Protocol (MCP)
resources.description: The open standard for connecting AI agents to tools and data sources. Think USB-C for AI. Browse 1000+ available servers.

resources.title: Advanced Agent Patterns
resources.description: Visual explainer: ReAct reasoning, Chain-of-Thought planning, multi-agent architectures, and MCP. The patterns that power production AI agents.

resources.title: ReAct Agent: Live Walkthrough
resources.description: Watch a ReAct agent think step-by-step: search, evaluate sources, extract facts, and synthesize a research report. Interactive trace with real data.

resources.title: Research Agent Workshop Notebook
resources.description: Build a research agent with ReAct reasoning, source evaluation, and report generation. Extends what you built in Session 4.

resources.title: Claude Code
resources.description: Anthropic's agentic coding tool. A real-world example of everything we discussed: ReAct reasoning, tool use, multi-step planning, MCP.

resources.title: ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting
resources.description: The original research paper that introduced the ReAct pattern. Showed that interleaving reasoning and action dramatically improves agent performance.

resources.title: Anthropic Cookbook: Tool Use Patterns
resources.description: Production-ready code examples for building agents with Claude. Covers tool definition, multi-tool orchestration, and error handling.

resources.title: Building Effective Agents (Anthropic)
resources.description: Anthropic's guide to building effective agents. Practical patterns, common pitfalls, and when to use agents vs. simpler solutions.

activities.title: Build a Research Agent
activities.description: 30 minutes in Google Colab. Build an agent that uses ReAct reasoning to research, evaluate, and report:

steps.title: Build the Tools (8 min)
steps.description: Define three tools: web_search (finds information), evaluate_source (rates credibility), and extract_key_facts (pulls structured data from text).

steps.title: Wire the ReAct Loop (10 min)
steps.description: Connect the tools to Claude with a reasoning loop. The agent thinks about what to search, evaluates what it finds, and decides if it needs more information.

steps.title: Add Report Generation (12 min)
steps.description: The agent synthesizes all findings into a structured briefing: key facts, source quality ratings, confidence level, and recommended next steps.

steps.title: Test with a Real Question
steps.description: Ask your agent to research a real business question: market size, competitor analysis, or industry trend. Evaluate the output quality.

codeBlock.code:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  NAIVE AGENT                                │
│                                             │
│  User: "Is the EV market growing?"          │
│  → search("EV market")                      │
│  → "Here's what I found: [raw dump]"        │
│  ❌ No evaluation. No reasoning. Just noise.│
└─────────────────────────────────────────────┘

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  ReAct AGENT                                │
│                                             │
│  User: "Is the EV market growing?"          │
│  THINK: Need market size data + growth rate │
│  → search("global EV market size 2025")     │
│  OBSERVE: Found IEA report, Bloomberg data  │
│  THINK: IEA is credible. Need growth trend. │
│  → search("EV sales growth rate YoY")       │
│  OBSERVE: 35% YoY growth, slowing in EU     │
│  THINK: Have enough for a solid answer.     │
│  → "EV market: $500B, +35% YoY, but..."    │
│  ✅ Reasoned. Evaluated. Synthesized.       │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────┘

activities.title: Multi-Agent Design Challenge
activities.description: Design (on paper) a multi-agent system for a real business process. Think like an architect:

steps.title: Pick a Business Process
steps.description: Choose one: customer support ticket handling, weekly competitor report, content publishing pipeline, or employee onboarding.

steps.title: Define Your Agents
steps.description: Name each agent, define its specialty, list its tools, and describe when it hands off to the next agent. Minimum 3 agents.

steps.title: Draw the Flow
steps.description: Sketch the pipeline on paper or whiteboard: triggers, handoffs, decision points, and human review gates.

steps.title: Present to Another Team (3 min)
steps.description: Walk another team through your design. They ask: Where could this fail? What needs human oversight? What's the ROI?

codeBlock.code:
┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐
│  RESEARCHER  │───>│    WRITER    │───>│   EDITOR     │
│              │    │              │    │              │
│ Tools:       │    │ Tools:       │    │ Tools:       │
│ • web_search │    │ • draft_doc  │    │ • check_facts│
│ • read_pdf   │    │ • format     │    │ • check_tone │
│ • summarize  │    │ • cite       │    │ • score      │
│              │    │              │    │              │
│ Output:      │    │ Output:      │    │ Output:      │
│ Research     │    │ Draft        │    │ Final report │
│ brief        │    │ report       │    │ + score      │
└──────────────┘    └──────────────┘    └──────────────┘
        │                                      │
        │          ┌──────────────┐             │
        └─────────>│  COORDINATOR │<────────────┘
                   │              │
                   │ Decides:     │
                   │ • Good enough?│
                   │ • Redo step? │
                   │ • Send to    │
                   │   human?     │
                   └──────────────┘

activities.title: Automation ROI Assessment
activities.description: Apply the Automation Matrix to a real business process and calculate potential ROI:

steps.title: Pick a Process You Know
steps.description: Choose a repetitive business task from your job, internship, or a company you admire. Something done at least weekly.

steps.title: Map It on the Automation Matrix
steps.description: Plot it on two axes: Volume (how often?) and Judgment Required (how much human thinking?). Decide: Full automation, augmentation, or human-only?

steps.title: Calculate the ROI
steps.description: Estimate: Hours per week x hourly cost x 52 weeks = annual cost. Subtract agent cost. Factor in error reduction. What's the payback period?

steps.title: Identify the Risks
steps.description: What could go wrong? What's the worst-case failure? Where do you need a human-in-the-loop checkpoint?

takeaways.text: ReAct = Think before you act. The best agents don't just execute — they reason about what to do, observe results, and adapt. This pattern separates toy demos from production systems.
Agent teams beat solo agents. Just like human organizations, specialized agents collaborating on complex tasks produce better results than one agent trying to do everything.
MCP is the USB-C of AI. The Model Context Protocol means you build a tool integration once and every agent can use it. This standard is reshaping how agents connect to the world.
Automate the routine, augment the judgment. The Automation Matrix helps you decide what to fully automate, what to augment with AI, and what must stay human.
Trust scales as stakes shrink. Give agents full autonomy on low-risk tasks. Keep humans in the loop for high-stakes decisions. The trust equation is your guide.

homework.label: Build:
homework.text: Extend your Colab research agent with a 4th tool (e.g., competitor_lookup or financial_data). Test it with 3 different business research questions and document the reasoning traces.

homework.label: Design:
homework.text: Write a 1-page multi-agent proposal for a business process you care about. Include: agent roles, tools needed, handoff logic, human review gates, and estimated ROI.

homework.label: Explore:
homework.text: Browse the MCP server directory at modelcontextprotocol.io. Find 3 servers relevant to your final project idea. Write one paragraph on how each could help.

homework.label: Reflect:
homework.text: Read Anthropic's 'Building Effective Agents' guide. Write 3 key takeaways and one thing that surprised you about when NOT to use agents.

nextSession.title: Session 9: Ethics & Responsible AI
nextSession.description: Agents are powerful. But power needs guardrails. Bias, fairness, privacy, and the responsible deployment of AI systems.
